metadata.summary: Docs-as-Code treats documentation as versioned code co-located with source, auto-generated where possible, validated in CI. ADRs hand-authored; API refs, changelogs, and links automated.

content.title: Living Documentation
content.summary: Living Documentation (Docs-as-Code) treats documentation as a versioned artifact co-located with source code, auto-generated from code where possible, validated in CI, and never manually edited in the auto-generated sections. ADRs and design rationale remain hand-authored. API reference, changelogs, and link validity are automated. Every auto-generated section is tagged `` so agents and humans know not to overwrite it manually.

why.p: Static wikis become graveyards within months. Docs that live outside version control drift from the code they describe. Broken links and stale examples erode trust. The Docs-as-Code model applies the same review, CI, and versioning discipline to documentation that code already has — documentation failures block deploys the same as test failures.

list.item: Setting up a new project's documentation pipeline: generator, CI pipeline, auto-generated API reference
When documentation has drifted from code: regenerate API reference from OpenAPI spec
When onboarding a new agent: living docs (especially llms.txt and structured README) reduce hallucination
When deploying a developer portal that surfaces service ownership and runbooks
Internal ADRs and design rationale — must remain manually authored; auto-generated "why" is always wrong
User-facing marketing copy — optimized for accuracy, not persuasion
Projects to be archived within 3 months — infrastructure investment exceeds value
Teams without code review discipline — Docs-as-Code requires the same PR review rigor as code PRs

rule.statement: Store documentation in the same repository as the code it describes, updated in the same PR.
rule.rationale: Co-location ensures the same review process catches doc drift. Separate wikis always lag behind code changes.

rule.statement: Tag every auto-generated section with `<!-- AUTO-GENERATED: source=openapi.yaml -->`. Never edit content inside AUTO-GENERATED boundaries manually or with an agent.
rule.rationale: Auto-generated sections are overwritten on the next generation pass. Manual edits inside them are silently lost. The tag makes the boundary explicit for both humans and agents.

rule.statement: Documentation failures (broken links, invalid API spec, failed build) must block the CI pipeline the same way test failures do.
rule.rationale: Documentation that does not block deploys is treated as optional. Optional docs are not maintained.

list.item: Auto-generate: API reference (from OpenAPI/JSDoc), changelogs (from conventional commits), code examples (from tests)
Hand-author: Architecture Decision Records, design rationale, user guides, tutorials, onboarding docs

antipattern.description: Agent overwrites an entire docs page on re-run, including manually-authored introduction sections.
antipattern.reason: Without AUTO-GENERATED tags, agents have no boundary to respect. Use explicit section markers and instruct agents to only update content between those tags.

rule.statement: Write `llms.txt` at the repo root with one-line descriptions of top-level doc sections. Do not copy full section content into llms.txt — link to full pages instead.
rule.rationale: Agents and LLM tools use llms.txt as a navigation index, not as full content. Over-detailed llms.txt files waste context budget on the index itself.

list.item: MkDocs + Material: Python projects, technical docs, fast builds, Material theme
Docusaurus: Node.js/React projects, developer portals, MDX support
VitePress: Vue projects, very fast builds
Starlight (Astro): modern DX, content collections with Zod schemas

rule.statement: Run `mkdocs build --strict` (or equivalent `--fail-on-warning`) in CI to catch broken references before deploy.
rule.rationale: Non-strict builds silently skip broken includes and missing pages. Strict mode surfaces all issues at build time.

rule.statement: Add three linting steps to the docs CI job: prose lint (Vale), link validation (linkinator), and API spec validation (Spectral for OpenAPI).
rule.rationale: Each tool catches a different failure class: Vale catches style drift, linkinator catches broken URLs, Spectral catches spec regressions. Running all three is the minimum viable doc quality gate.

antipattern.description: Running Vale with default rules on technical writing without customization.
antipattern.reason: Default Vale rules produce too many false positives on technical prose (passive voice warnings, capitalization rules). Configure `.vale.ini` and disable overly aggressive rules before using in CI.

antipattern.description: Agents updating OpenAPI specs without running a breaking-change check.
antipattern.reason: Renamed fields and removed endpoints are breaking changes. Run `redocly diff` or `bump.sh diff` before committing spec changes to surface regressions.

template.purpose: GitHub Actions workflow: lint, link check, API spec validate, build, deploy
MkDocs Material theme configuration for technical docs
